claim: The zodiac section's anomalously low entropy (3.7149 bits, lowest of all sections) reflects a label-only encoding where Voynich words are one-to-one mappings to a closed set of astrological or calendrical terms (month names, zodiac signs, star names), rather than running prose, making it the most tractable section for frequency-matching decipherment.
evidence_for: Zodiac section has only 1,297 words across 12 folios â€” approximately 108 words/folio vs 84 for herbal â€” consistent with sparse labeling
Low entropy (3.7149 bits) is consistent with a constrained closed-class vocabulary
Prior belief [0.616] already partially supports this interpretation
12 folios mapping to 12 zodiac signs gives ~108 words each, plausibly 12-15 unique labels per sign repeated across nymphs or divisions
evidence_against: Label-only encoding would predict even lower type-token ratio than observed
If labels are in an unknown language or use homophones, frequency matching to known astrological term sets will fail
test: Compute the type-token ratio (TTR) and rank-frequency slope for zodiac section words. Compare TTR and slope to: (a) a synthetic label corpus constructed from Latin zodiac/calendar terminology repeated with nymph-count weighting, and (b) the herbal section as a prose baseline.
test_metric: TTR and Zipf slope for zodiac vs. synthetic label corpus vs. herbal prose corpus
test_threshold: If zodiac TTR falls within 1 SD of the synthetic label corpus TTR (expected ~0.25-0.40 for 1,297 tokens) and differs from herbal by > 2 SD, label-encoding hypothesis is supported

tests_run.test: compute the type-token ratio (ttr) and rank-frequency slope for zodiac section words. compare ttr an
tests_run.details: Observed Zipf exponent: 0.8946 (R²=0.9084). Natural language expected: ~1.0. Fit quality: moderate.

reasoning: A confirmed label structure in zodiac would provide a constrained 12-24 word partial plaintext, enabling frequency-attack bootstrapping for the rest of the manuscript â€” the highest leverage single finding available.
